dependencies.explanation: In TypeScript projects using @typescript-eslint, many ESLint rules are type-aware (e.g., no-unsafe-assignment, no-unsafe-call, no-floating-promises). Type errors and type-unsafe patterns (like overuse of `any` to suppress tsc errors) directly generate ESLint violations from these rules. Reducing tsc_errors — especially by adding proper type annotations and eliminating `any` — will mechanically reduce a subset of eslint_violations without any separate ESLint-specific effort.

React/TypeScript projects of this structure (oksskolten uses TSX and has a full component library) almost universally configure eslint-plugin-prettier, which surfaces every Prettier formatting deviation as an ESLint violation. This means prettier_violations is a direct subset contributor to eslint_violations. Running `prettier --write` on all files would eliminate this entire category of ESLint violations in a single automated pass, providing a high-leverage, zero-effort fix.

ESLint violations and tsc errors tend to co-accumulate in the same files and are symptoms of the same underlying discipline gap. Files with poor type discipline (triggering ESLint @typescript-eslint rules) are the same files likely to carry actual tsc errors. Resolving one category often uncovers or motivates fixing the other, as developers touch those files.

No direct technical link exists between type correctness and formatting. However, both metrics accumulating simultaneously is a signal that automated quality gates (pre-commit hooks, CI checks) are not being enforced. If tsc_errors are high, it suggests the CI type-check gate is either absent or ignored, which is the same condition that allows prettier_violations to grow unchecked.

Dockerfile issues are technically isolated from TypeScript source quality, but both metrics reflect the same root cause: the absence of enforced linting gates in CI. A project permitting Hadolint violations is likely also permitting ESLint violations to merge, suggesting a systemic CI enforcement gap rather than targeted technical debt.

risk_clusters.risk: Runtime correctness and type safety risk. This is the highest-severity cluster. TypeScript errors indicate the compiler cannot verify type contracts, meaning incorrect data shapes, null dereferences, or broken API assumptions may reach production. Because @typescript-eslint rules are type-aware, these two metrics reinforce each other — unresolved type errors cause developers to introduce `any` escapes, which in turn generates more ESLint violations. This cluster is especially dangerous in a large component codebase (80+ TSX/TS files across article, chat, feed, settings, and UI layers) where incorrect prop types or hook return types can silently propagate across the component tree.

Maintainability and code review friction risk. Inconsistent formatting across a large codebase (this project has files across 8+ feature domains) creates noisy diffs, slows code review, and increases merge conflict surface area. If eslint-plugin-prettier is active, this cluster also inflates the ESLint violation count artificially, masking the true severity of logic-level ESLint issues. The entire cluster can be resolved in one automated pass (`prettier --write` + `eslint --fix`), making it high-leverage but deceptively costly if left to accumulate further.

Container security and deployment reliability risk. Hadolint violations in the Dockerfile may indicate unpinned base image tags (supply chain risk), missing USER directives (privilege escalation risk), inefficient layer ordering (slow builds, large images), or missing HEALTHCHECK directives (orchestration reliability). This cluster is isolated from source code quality but is a deployment gate risk — a single misconfigured Dockerfile affects every environment the container is deployed to.

doc_understanding.application_purpose: Oksskolten is a self-hosted, AI-native RSS reader that automatically fetches and extracts full article text for every feed item by default — no per-feed configuration required. It exists to provide a complete reading experience with built-in AI summarization, translation, and chat, eliminating the need to click through to source sites.
target_users: Self-hosters and homelab operators who want a privacy-respecting, locally-run RSS reader
Developers and technical users comfortable with Docker Compose deployment
Heavy RSS consumers who want AI-assisted reading (summarization, translation, Q&A) without a SaaS subscription
MCP/Claude Code users who want to query their article archive programmatically
key_workflows: Subscribe to and manage RSS/Atom feeds with auto-discovery
Automatically fetch and extract full article text from source URLs on every poll cycle
Read full articles in-app without navigating to the original site
On-demand AI summarization and translation of articles (Anthropic, Gemini, or OpenAI)
Multi-turn AI chat over the article archive via MCP tooling
Full-text search across entire article archive via Meilisearch
Clip arbitrary URLs as articles with full content extraction
Authenticate via password, Passkey/WebAuthn, or GitHub OAuth
Offline reading via PWA with background sync
Bypass bot-protection on paywalled or JS-rendered sites via FlareSolverr
Access sites without RSS using RSS Bridge or LLM inference
known_limitations: Single-container, single-process architecture with SQLite means no horizontal scaling or multi-instance deployment — explicitly incompatible with serverless/edge runtimes
Adaptive feed scheduling interval range is fixed at 15 min–4 h; no user-configurable override per feed
FlareSolverr fallback for JS-rendered/bot-protected sites requires an additional Docker sidecar and may not bypass all protections
No multi-user support documented — appears to be a single-user or small-team self-hosted app with no per-user feed isolation or role-based access
AI provider keys are configured through the Settings UI, implying they are stored in the database rather than exclusively in environment variables — potential operational/security concern
Roadmap indicates orphaned image purging does not yet exist, meaning deleted article images accumulate and consume disk indefinitely
No storage usage dashboard exists yet — disk consumption from cached images is not visible to the user
Offline PWA support relies on background sync, which may not work consistently across all browsers or when the server is unreachable for extended periods
Rate-limited feeds (429/503) respect Retry-After but feeds are never permanently disabled — dead/defunct feeds must be manually removed
Fly.io + Turso deployment requires external libsql (Turso) instead of local SQLite, adding a cloud dependency to an otherwise self-contained stack
improvement_opportunities: Orphaned image cleanup is planned but unimplemented: add a scheduled background job (e.g., via the existing node-cron scheduler) that periodically scans stored image URLs against the articles table and deletes unreferenced blobs, preventing unbounded disk growth.
No storage usage dashboard exists: add a settings/admin page showing total image cache size, image count, and per-feed breakdown — the roadmap explicitly calls this out as desired but it is missing.

Dead feed handling is passive — feeds are never automatically disabled even after sustained errors: add a configurable threshold (e.g., 20 consecutive errors over 7 days) that flags or disables feeds and surfaces them in a 'dead feeds' management UI so users can review and remove them.

No multi-user support is documented: adding user accounts with per-user feed lists and read-state would make Oksskolten viable for shared household or team use, a common homelab scenario given its single-container deployment model.

Feed scheduling intervals (15 min–4 h) are fully automatic with no user override: expose a per-feed 'check interval override' field in the feed settings UI so power users can force high-frequency polling for time-sensitive feeds or reduce polling for low-priority ones.

AI provider API keys are configured through the Settings UI (implying DB storage): document and support an environment-variable-only mode for secret injection at deploy time, preventing credentials from being stored in the SQLite database and aligning with 12-factor/secrets-management best practices.
No bulk OPML export endpoint is mentioned: add an OPML export function so users can migrate feeds out or back up their subscription list — a standard expectation for any RSS reader.

Error messages and extraction failure states are not described as surfacing remediation hints to the user: when Readability extraction fails (e.g., paywalled content, login walls), show an actionable in-app message such as 'Enable FlareSolverr for this feed' or 'Add a CSS selector override' rather than silently showing an empty article.

The MCP server integration is mentioned but not documented in the README beyond a single sentence: add a dedicated MCP setup guide (tool list, example prompts, authentication method) so users know how to connect Claude Code or other MCP clients to their Oksskolten instance.

No webhook or notification system is documented: add outbound webhook support (or at least ntfy/Apprise integration) to notify users of new articles matching saved search filters, enabling use as a monitoring/alerting feed reader without requiring the user to actively open the app.

proposals.title: Storage Usage Dashboard in Settings Data Tab
proposals.target_outcome: Users can see total image cache size, image count, and a per-feed breakdown directly in the Settings Data tab, enabling them to identify and reason about disk consumption before it becomes a problem.

Data is fetched via the same SWR/fetcher pattern used throughout the app. No schema changes are required — the data comes from querying the existing image storage backend.

proposals.title: Dead Feed Detection Banner and Bulk-Remove Action
proposals.target_outcome: Users are proactively shown which feeds have been consistently failing and can remove them in bulk from a dedicated 'dead feeds' section, preventing silent accumulation of broken subscriptions.

This proposal adds a collapsible 'Dead Feeds' section at the bottom of the sidebar feed list that groups feeds exceeding a threshold (e.g., consecutive_errors > 20 or last_fetched > 7 days with error status). Each dead feed shows its last error type and a remove button. A 'Remove All Dead Feeds' bulk action is added to `MultiSelectContextMenu`. All data to derive this view is already present in the feed API response — no schema changes are needed.

proposals.title: Actionable Extraction Failure Hints in Article Body
proposals.target_outcome: When full-text extraction fails (empty body, login wall, JS-rendered page), the article view shows a contextual hint explaining why and suggesting a concrete remediation step — such as enabling FlareSolverr or adding a CSS selector override — instead of silently showing a blank article.
proposals.description: Currently when Readability extraction fails or returns an empty body, the article content body renders as blank with no feedback. The document understanding identifies this as a pain point: users have no indication of what went wrong or how to fix it. The existing `HintBanner` component (`hint-banner.tsx`) and `classifyError` function in `feed-error-banner.tsx` already provide the building blocks. This proposal adds a detection step in `ArticleContentBody` that checks if the extracted content is empty or below a minimum threshold, then renders a `HintBanner` with a context-specific message. Hint variants include: 'This page may require JavaScript — try enabling FlareSolverr for this feed', 'This content appears to be behind a login wall', and 'Extraction produced no readable content — you can still read at the source'. The article toolbar's external link is highlighted in the hint for easy access.
acceptance_criteria: When an article has no extracted body content, a HintBanner is shown above the empty article area rather than a blank page

The hint includes a direct link to the original article URL for easy fallback
Articles with valid content are unaffected — no hint is shown when body content exists
The existing HintBanner dismiss behaviour works correctly so users can hide the hint after reading it
No new npm dependencies are introduced

tasks.title: Run Prettier autofix across all TypeScript/TSX source files
tasks.description:
This is a pure formatting task that requires no logic changes.

**Steps:**
1. Confirm a `.prettierrc` (or `prettier.config.js` / `prettier` key in `package.json`) is present at the repo root. If it is missing, create a minimal `.prettierrc` by running `npx prettier --write .prettierrc` — but only after confirming the existing CI check config (check `package.json` scripts and any `.github/` workflow files) to understand what options are expected.
2. Run the autofix: `npx prettier --write 'src/**/*.{ts,tsx}'`
3. Inspect the diff with `git diff --stat` to confirm only formatting changes (whitespace, quotes, semicolons, trailing commas) are present — no logic diffs should appear.
4. Run the full test suite to confirm no tests broke: `npm test` (or equivalent script in `package.json`).
5. Commit the result with message `chore: apply prettier autofix across src/`.

**Do NOT** modify any non-`src/` files, `.eslintrc`, or CI workflow files. If the prettier config is already present, step 1 is a no-op.

tasks.title: Pin apt-get package versions and add --no-install-recommends in Dockerfile
tasks.description:
Fix the two Hadolint violations on Dockerfile line 4 (DL3008 and DL3015).

**Steps:**
1. Identify the Debian base image version used in the Dockerfile (e.g., `debian:12` or `node:20-bookworm-slim`).
2. For each package listed in the `apt-get install` line, look up the current pinnable version. You can do this by running a throwaway container: `docker run --rm <base-image> apt-cache show <pkg> | grep '^Version:'`. Record the exact version string (e.g., `curl=7.88.1-10+deb12u5`).
3. Edit Dockerfile line 4:
   - Append `=<version>` to each package name.
   - Add `--no-install-recommends` immediately after `-y`.
   - Example result: `RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends curl=7.88.1-10+deb12u5 && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*`
4. Verify the build succeeds: `docker build .`
5. Run Hadolint to confirm zero warnings: `hadolint Dockerfile`

**Do NOT** change the base image tag, add or remove packages, modify any other Dockerfile instruction, or alter `docker-compose` files.

acceptance_criteria.criterion: Hadolint reports zero warnings and zero info messages for the Dockerfile
acceptance_criteria.expected_outcome: Exit code 0; no output (or only a blank line)

acceptance_criteria.criterion: Docker build completes successfully after the change
acceptance_criteria.expected_outcome: Exit code 0; build output ends with 'Successfully built' or equivalent BuildKit success message

safety_constraints: Do NOT modify the base image tag or change the Debian release
Do NOT add or remove packages from the apt-get install line
Do NOT modify any docker-compose.yml or docker-compose.override.yml files
Do NOT modify any src/ files
tasks.rollback_plan: git revert HEAD — restores the original Dockerfile. The only consequence is the two Hadolint warnings returning; no running containers are affected.

tasks.title: Add collapsible Dead Feeds section to sidebar with bulk-remove action
tasks.description:
Surface consistently-failing feeds in the sidebar and provide single and bulk removal.

**Implementation steps:**

1. **Define the dead-feed threshold.** In `src/components/feed/feed-list.tsx`, add a helper (or extend the existing `isFeedInactive`) that returns `true` when a feed object has `consecutive_errors > 20` OR (`last_fetched` is more than 7 days ago AND the feed has an error status). Extract this as a named constant `DEAD_FEED_THRESHOLD` so it is easy to adjust later.

2. **Add a `DeadFeedsSection` component** (can be a local sub-component inside `feed-list.tsx` or a new file at `src/components/feed/dead-feeds-section.tsx`):
   - Accept `deadFeeds: Feed[]` and `onRemove: (feedId: string) => void` props.
   - Render a collapsible section (use whatever accordion/disclosure primitive the app already has, or a simple `<details>`/`<summary>` if none exists).

   - The section header shows the count, e.g. 'Dead Feeds (3)'.
   - When `deadFeeds.length === 0`, render nothing (`null`).

3. **Wire removal.** The `onRemove` handler should call the existing feed-deletion API (same as the individual remove in `feed-context-menu.tsx`) and then call `mutate()` to revalidate the feed SWR cache.

4. **Add 'Remove All Dead Feeds' to multi-select context menu.** In `src/components/feed/feed-context-menu.tsx` (or wherever `MultiSelectContextMenu` is defined), add a 'Remove All Dead Feeds' menu item. This item should only appear when `deadFeeds.length > 0`. On click, it should iterate and call the delete API for each dead feed, then revalidate.

5. **Placement.** Render `<DeadFeedsSection>` at the bottom of the feed list, below all active feeds.

6. **No new npm dependencies.** Use existing UI primitives only.

tasks.title: Show contextual extraction-failure HintBanner in article body when content is empty
tasks.description:
Detect empty/failed extraction in `ArticleContentBody` and surface a context-sensitive `HintBanner` instead of a blank view.

**Implementation steps:**

1. **Add a content-empty detection helper** inside `src/components/article/article-content-body.tsx`:
   - Define a constant `MIN_CONTENT_LENGTH = 50` (characters) as the minimum threshold for 'valid' extracted content.
   - A helper `isContentEmpty(body: string | null | undefined): boolean` returns `true` when body is null/undefined/empty or `body.trim().length < MIN_CONTENT_LENGTH`.

2. **Classify the failure reason.** Inspect the article object (or props) for signals that indicate the failure type. Use or extend `classifyError` from `src/components/feed/feed-error-banner.tsx` if applicable. Map to one of three hint variants:
   - `'js-render'` — if the feed or article metadata indicates a JS-heavy site (e.g., a known signal in the article/feed object, or a heuristic on the URL). Message: _"This page may require JavaScript to render. Try enabling FlareSolverr for this feed."_
   - `'paywall'` — if error classification returns a paywall/auth signal. Message: _"This content appears to be behind a login wall. You can read it at the original source."_
   - `'generic'` — fallback for all other empty cases. Message: _"Full-text extraction produced no readable content. You can still read the article at the source."_

3. **Render the HintBanner.** In the JSX of `ArticleContentBody`, when `isContentEmpty(body)` is true:
   - Render `<HintBanner>` (from `src/components/ui/hint-banner.tsx`) above the (empty) article area.
   - Include a link to the article's `url` (original source) inside the hint, styled as a prominent CTA. This satisfies the requirement to highlight the toolbar's external link.
   - Preserve whatever dismiss behaviour `HintBanner` already has (e.g., a close button that sets local state to hide it).
   - When `isContentEmpty(body)` is false, render nothing extra — existing rendering is completely unchanged.

4. **Do not modify article toolbar logic** — the external link in the toolbar should remain as-is. The hint simply duplicates the URL as a convenience.

5. **No new npm dependencies.**

6. **Run Prettier** on modified files.

The HintBanner dismiss button (if present in the existing HintBanner component) works correctly — clicking it hides the hint for that article view
